text: I followed the math and built a cyclone filter to spec. It literally did absolutely nothing and let the tar get passed into my radiators. So anyone reading this, SKIP THE MATH on the cyclone filter and just make a condensation tank with a pipe down into it like he's saying.

To me it aint beautiful if it aint nailed, welded, screwed, glued, wired, duct taped and accidentally melted together in spots to add character to cans, bottles, old mufflers, hubcaps, couch springs and it is just amazing what a few car parts can do, even if they don't work they really draw curiosity from the brave bystanders who don't run while you're pushing your new heap down to the scrap yard to get some decent tires. So, from one contraption connoisseur to some British guy, keep up the great work brother. I'm almost sorry we beat ya'll in that war you started, but hey, you started it.

author: @danielbostrom129
text: On your cyclone, you have the outlet too close to the bottom. On most cyclones the outlet pipe only penetrates to about 1X the diameter of the housing below the inlet. If you have it go that close to the bottom as you have it set up it will re-entrain the particulate matter that was centrifugally separated.

author: @kristijanleitinger4478
text:
Hi Robert! I have been in the woodgas biusness for over a decade. I never coment anywhere on Youtube but l like your selfless way of thinking and shareing knolidge so l thod to do the same. 
Think a gasifier as carbourator. A precise, fine tooned machine designed to fit a specific engine. Its made to asure the engine runing optimal. As there exist good carbourators, there exist good wood gasifiers. But, you can allso run a engine with just a hose driping petrol in the intake manifold. Thats your FEMA 😄 it is not designed to run a engine for any long amount of time, just as a one time run in a emergancy. The problem is, it operates at too low temperature for all tars to crack and reduce so you end up with trace amounts of tar that collect on most turbolent parts of the engine - first on intake valves. No problem when engine is warm, but once cool the tar glues the valve still. 

Good news is this design is good for runing a engine for a few minutes as a test and it can be upgraded to a Imbert design, by adding air nozzles and flow restrictor plates (to compress the heat down in a tight turbolent space). 

There is the site called DriveOnWood that is the best woodgas information center in the world. 

And be carefull, woodgas is adictive. Once you hear a engine run on wood you are hooked for life.

author: @donwright3427
text: Fuel needs to be 100 percent dry and of uniform size.Even slightly damp wood will kill the process. Uneven size chunks will bridge and a shaker will need to be employed. If the gas produced is not clean the intake valves of your engine will gum up really quickly. Dont be fooled into thinking it's easy to do and beware as there are many videos on U tube which are missleading.

author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
text:
Hi Robert, you want your filter stage after cooling. If the gas is hot then nothing will  drop in your filter. The Cyclone is nothing more than a coarse filter to keep your cooler from clogging up it just get the coarse dust out. I quit building those years ago as they are not very effective and we pull the gas out in a way where it does not drag all that dust with it. After this coarse filter you cool the gas as much as you possibly can. Then run through your fine filter. A suggestion for you coming from years of rapid development is to build a bag filter made of silica cloth. This cloth can withstand very high temperature and is exempt from corrosion of the gas. You can add additional media to this filter if you wish (sort of use it like a basket). But the idea of the bag filter is build a "cake" on the filter. The bag is not the filter it is the cake build up that becomes the filter. The dirtier it gets the better it will clean and they breath very well. A clogged up filter will do more harm than good. If they become too restrictive, your gasifier wont be able to breath and thoroughly process the gas and the resulting tar will pass right thru that filter an into your engine. 

To make the bag filter all you need is a stapler to staple the seems to create the bag. Look up a " duct flange" you can attach that to a bucket lid of sorts and then use the lip of the flange to clamp on the bag filter using a band clamp. Sort of like a shop vacuum cleaner system.

replies.author: @Thrive-Off-Grid
replies.text:
@weld-deluxltd7635 My best advice to any new builder is to build a charcoal unit. Once you learn this you most likely will never even bother with a direct wood gas system. Charcoal is by far easier fuel to produce, they are stupid simple to make, they crack water directly making up for a lot of the energy losses making the fuel. They run 100% more reliable with no automation required. 

A direct wood gasifier will need automation in order for it to self sustain. I dont care how good of  a builder you are physics is physics and it can not be overcome with out automation. Direct wood gasification is problematic. You can build a charcoal unit and actually use it or you can spend hours and hours and lots of money building a and fiddling with a direct wood gasifier. Ive built some of the most advanced system to ever exist and even I am drooping that tech. Later systems will use a hybrid system where there will be a direct wood gasifier that can run in a full heating mode. Yes this will be installed inside the home. I have the reactor tech that can make that possible. However this will also have a charcoal gasifier unit to scrub and crack that gas. When the stove is in gasifier mode, it will work just like any other gasifier but the gas will be piped into the charcoal unit. The charcoal unit will crack all the moisture and tars produced in that gas, plus add in additional gas from its process. The stove like any gasifier will produce char ash. That char ash is what will be used in the charcoal unit. So it will be totally self sustaining without need to produce fuel for the charcoal unit as the stove is doing this already for free and that char ash is no longer going to waste.

replies.author: @weld-deluxltd7635
replies.text: @Thrive-Off-Grid Once again thank you for the information! I have subscribed to your channel, so you will be seeing a lot more questions from me :)   I am interested to build a plant for the production of activated charcoal, so i need to look through all of your videos and educate myself. I agree with you that gasifiers are challenging as i have customers who run them for commercial power and heat generations and they are non stop maintenance hassle. My profession is welding so if you ever need advice, a second opinion, or anything else, i will be glad to help!

author: @JSabh
text: Looks great. Temp is the best way to clean the gas. Yes, I have seen the FEMA version as well and adaptations but, in my humble opinion, using a sealed vessel and heating it from the outside is best due to the reduced flash back that is possible with the system you are designing but, you would then need two sealed vessels to keep the reaction organic or use a separate source, like a wood fire, to keep the reaction going. The temps are 600 F and the asphalt ( heavies ) fall out of flow, 400 F diesel and kerosene will condensate out of flow, at 200 F gasoline will condensate out. Beyond that, you will get the propane, methane, ect. and eventually just Hydrogen and Carbon Monoxide aka syngas.  Hope it goes well, cheers.

author: @AtlasReburdened
text: Just in case no one has mentioned this yet. Make sure you have something very amply venting welding fumes outside if you're welding on galvanized steel. The vaporized zinc will mess you up. Welders flu is no fun.
